volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Captives (Section 3.4.587 - Section 3.4.651b)

sections.label: Section 3.4.587
sections.original: Scio te id nolle fieri ; efficiam tamen ego id , si di adiuvant .
sections.translation: I know that you don ’ t wish that to be done ; still I shall effect it , if the Gods assist me . I shall bring him back here , and he will restore me to my father , in Elis . For that purpose have I sent Tyndarus hence to my father .

sections.label: Section 3.4.589b
sections.original: Quin tute is es :
sections.translation: Why , you yourself are he ;

sections.label: Section 3.4.590
sections.original: neque praeter te in Alide ullus servos istoc nominest .
sections.translation: nor is there any slave in Elis of that name , except yourself .

sections.label: Section 3.4.591
sections.original: Pergin servom me exprobrare esse , id quod vi hostili optigit ?
sections.translation: Do you persist in reproaching me with being a slave — a thing that has befallen me through the fortune of war ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.592
sections.original: Enim iam nequeo contineri .
sections.translation: Really , now , I cannot contain myself .

sections.label: Section 3.4.592b
sections.original: Heus , audin quid ait ? quin fugis ?
sections.translation: Ha ! don ’ t you hear him ? Why don ’ t you take to flight ? He ’ ll be pelting us just now with stones there , unless you order him to be seized .

sections.label: Section 3.4.594b
sections.original: Crucior .
sections.translation: I ’ m distracted .

sections.label: Section 3.4.594c
sections.original: Ardent oculi : fit opus , Hegio ;
sections.translation: His eyes strike fire ; there ’ s need of a rope , Hegio .

sections.label: Section 3.4.595
sections.original: viden tu illi maculari corpus totum maculis luridis ?
sections.translation: Don ’ t you see how his body is spotted all over with livid spots ? Black bile [*] is disordering the man .

sections.label: Line 596b
sections.original: At pol te , si hic sapiat senex ,
sections.translation: And , by my faith , if this old gentleman is wise , black pitch [*] will be disordering you with the executioner , and giving a light to your head .

sections.label: Section 3.4.598
sections.original: Iam deliramenta loquitur , laruae stimulant virum .
sections.translation: He ’ s now talking in his fit of delirium ; sprites are in possession of the man .

sections.label: Section 3.4.599
sections.original: hercle qui , si hunc comprehendi iusseris , sapias magis .
sections.translation: By my troth , suppose I order him to be seized ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.600
sections.original: Crucior , lapidem non habere mé , ut illi mastigiae
sections.translation: I ’ m vexed that I haven ’ t a stone , to knock out the brains of that whip-scoundrel , who ’ s driving me to madness by his taunts .

sections.label: Section 3.4.602
sections.original: Audin lapidem quaeritare ?
sections.translation: Don ’ t you hear that he ’ s looking for a stone ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.602b
sections.original: Solus te solum volo ,
sections.translation: I wish to speak with you alone , separately , Hegio .

sections.label: Section 3.4.603b
sections.original: Istinc loquere , si quid vis , procul . tamen audiam .
sections.translation: Speak from where you are , if you want anything ; though at a distance , I shall hear you .

sections.label: Section 3.4.604
sections.original: Namque edepol si adbites propius , os denasabit tibi
sections.translation: Yes , for , by my faith , if you approach nearer , he ’ ll be taking your nose off with his teeth .

sections.label: Section 3.4.605b
sections.original: Neque pol me insanum , Hégio , esse creduis
sections.translation: By heavens , Hegio , don ’ t you believe that I am mad , or that I ever was so , or that I have the malady which that fellow avers . But if you fear anything from me , order me to be bound ; I wish it , so long as that fellow is bound as well .

sections.label: Section 3.4.608b
sections.original: Immo enim vero , Hegio ,
sections.translation: Why really , Hegio , rather let him be bound that wishes it .

sections.label: Section 3.4.609b
sections.original: Tace modo . ego te , Philocrates
sections.translation: Now hold your tongue ! I ’ ll make you , you false Philocrates , to be found out this day to be a real Tyndarus .

sections.label: Section 3.4.611
sections.original: quid mi abnutas ?
sections.translation: Why are you making signs [*] at me ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.611b
sections.original: Tibi ego abnuto ?
sections.translation: I , making signs at you ? ( To HEGIO .) What would he do , if you were at a greater distance off ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.612
sections.original: Quid ais ? quid si adeam hunc insanum ?
sections.translation: What do you say ? What if I approach this madman ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.612b
sections.original: Nugas . ludificabitur ,
sections.translation: Nonsense ; you ’ ll be made a fool of ; he ’ ll be prating stuff , to you , neither the feet nor the head of which will ever be visible .

sections.label: Section 3.4.615
sections.original: ornamenta absunt : Aiacem , hunc cum vides , ipsum vides .
sections.translation: The dress only [*] is wanting ; in seeing this man , you behold Ajax himself .

sections.label: Section 3.4.616
sections.original: Nihili facio . tamen adibo .
sections.translation: I don ’ t care ; still I ’ ll approach him .

sections.label: Section 3.4.616b
sections.original: Nunc ego omnino occidi ,
sections.translation: Now am I utterly undone ; now between the sacrifice and the stone [*] do I stand , nor know I what to do .

sections.label: Section 3.4.618
sections.original: Do tibi operam , Aristophontes , si quid est quod me velis .
sections.translation: I lend you my attention , Aristophontes , if there is anything that you would wish with me .

sections.label: Section 3.4.619
sections.original: Ex me audibis vera quae nunc falsa opinare , Hegio .
sections.translation: From me you shall hear that truth , which now you think to be false , Hegio .

sections.label: Section 3.4.620
sections.original: sed hoc primúm , me expurigare tibi volo , me insaniam
sections.translation: But I wish , in the first place , to clear myself from this with you — that madness does not possess me , and that I have no malady , except that I am in captivity ; and , so may the King of Gods and of men make me to regain my native land , that fellow there is no more Philocrates than either I or you .

sections.label: Section 3.4.623b
sections.original: Eho dic mihi ,
sections.translation: Come , then , tell me who he is ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.624b
sections.original: Quem dudum dixi a principio tibi .
sections.translation: He whom I ’ ve told you all along from the beginning .

sections.label: Section 3.4.625
sections.original: hoc si secus reperies , nullam causam dico quin mihi
sections.translation: If you shall find him any other than that person , I show no cause why I shouldn ’ t suffer the loss with you both of my parents and of my liberty for ever .

sections.label: Section 3.4.627
sections.original: Quid tu ais ?
sections.translation: What say you to this ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.627b
sections.original: Me tuom esse servom et te meum erum .
sections.translation: That I am your slave , and you my master .

sections.label: Section 3.4.627c
sections.original: Haud istuc rogo .
sections.translation: I didn ’ t ask that — were you a free man ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.628b
sections.original: Fui .
sections.translation: I was .

sections.label: Section 3.4.628c
sections.original: Enim vero non fuit , nugas agit .
sections.translation: But he really wasn ’ t ; he is deceiving you .

sections.label: Section 3.4.629
sections.original: Qui tu scis ? an tu fortasse fuisti meae matri obstetrix ,
sections.translation: How do you know ? Were you , perchance , the midwife of my mother ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.630
sections.original: qui id tam audacter dicere audes ?
sections.translation: since you dare to affirm this so boldly ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.630b
sections.original: Puerum te vidi puer .
sections.translation: When a boy , I saw yourself , a boy .

sections.label: Section 3.4.631
sections.original: At ego te video maior maiorem : ém rursum tibi .
sections.translation: But , grown up , I now see you grown up ; so , there ’ s for you , in return . If you did right , you wouldn ’ t be troubling yourself about my concerns ; do I trouble myself about yours ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.633
sections.original: Fuitne huic patér Thensaurochrysonicochrysides ?
sections.translation: Was his father called Thesaurochrysonicocrœsides ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.634
sections.original: Non fuit , neque ego istuc nomen umquam audivi ante hunc diem .
sections.translation: He was not ; and I never heard that name before this day .

sections.label: Section 3.4.635
sections.original: Philocrati Theodoromedes fuit pater .
sections.translation: Theodoromedes was the father of Philocrates .

sections.label: Section 3.4.635b
sections.original: Pereo probe .
sections.translation: I ’ m downright undone . Why don ’ t you be quiet , heart of mine ? Go and be stretched , and hang yourself ; you are throbbing so , that unfortunate I can hardly stand up for my fear .

sections.label: Section 3.4.638
sections.original: Satin istuc mihi exquisitum est , fuisse hunc servom in Alide
sections.translation: Is a full assurance given me that this was a slave in Elis , and that he is not Philocrates ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.639b
sections.original: Tam satis quam numquam hoc invenies secus .
sections.translation: So fully , that you will never find this to be otherwise ; but where is he [*] now ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.640b
sections.original: Vbi ego minime atque ipsus se volt maxume .
sections.translation: Where I the least , and he the most could wish himself . In consequence , then , I ’ m cut asunder [*] , disjointed , to my sorrow , by the devices of this scoundrel , who has bamboozled me by his tricks just as he has thought fit . But do , please , have a care that you are right .

sections.label: Section 3.4.643b
sections.original: Quin exploratum dico et provisum hoc tibi .
sections.translation: Why , I assure you of this , as an ascertained and established fact .

sections.label: Section 3.4.644
sections.original: Certon ?
sections.translation: For certain ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.644b
sections.original: Quin nihil , inquam , invenies magis hoc certo certius .
sections.translation: Why , nothing , I say , will you find more certain than this certainty .

sections.label: Section 3.4.645
sections.original: Philocrates iam inde usque amicus fuit mihi a puero puer .
sections.translation: Philocrates , from when a boy , has ever since that time been my friend .

sections.label: Section 3.4.646
sections.original: sed qua faciest tuos sodalis Philocrates ?
sections.translation: But of what appearance is your friend Philocrates ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.646b
sections.original: Dicam tibi :
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell you :

sections.label: Section 3.4.647
sections.original: macilento ore , naso acuto , corpore albo , oculis nigris ,
sections.translation: with a thin face , sharp nose , light hair , dark eyes , somewhat ruddy , with hair rather crisp and curling ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.648b
sections.original: Convenit .
sections.translation: The description is like .

sections.label: Section 3.4.649
sections.original: Vt quidem hercle in medium ego hodie pessume processerim .
sections.translation: Aye , so much so , indeed , that I ’ ve this day , much to my sorrow , got into the midst of this , i ’ faith . Woe to those unfortunate rods which this day will be meeting their end upon my back .

sections.label: Section 3.4.651
sections.original: Verba mihi data esse video .
sections.translation: I see that I ’ ve been imposed upon .

sections.label: Section 3.4.651b
sections.original: Quid cessatis , compedes ,
sections.translation: Why , fetters , do you delay to run towards me and to embrace my legs , that I may have you in custody ?
